pros: Great benefits with unlimited PTO, $1000 Wellbeing Fund, flexible remote work. Busy season (usually about 3 months) requires working overtime but the rest of the year is pretty standard hours. cons: Niche work with not a lot of room for growth or creativity in your first few years. You’ll mostly just be documenting things with little understanding of why (unless you really go out of your way to ask/research/find out), but most teams are too busy to explain things in detail anyway so it’s difficult to learn from them. Also, pay-wise (imo) it’s not really worth the hours once you pass Staff 2. As a senior you’re expected to work 50-60 hrs on a normal week and even more in busy season, but your raise is not that much and you can definitely make more money elsewhere with 2-3 years of experience.

title: -Prestigious Brand & Credibility: Having “EY” on your résumé opens doors and signals that you can operate in large, complex environments and adhere to professional processes. -Client & Stakeholder Management -People at my level are nice -Network Expansion: Collaborating across business units and geographies has broadened my professional network both internally and with clients. -Decent benefits
pros:
-Prestigious Brand & Credibility: Having “EY” on your résumé opens doors and signals that you can operate in large, complex environments and adhere to professional processes.

-Client & Stakeholder Management

-People at my level are nice

-Network Expansion: Collaborating across business units and geographies has broadened my professional network both internally and with clients.

-Decent benefits
cons:
-Backward Stance on AI: ChatGPT and other best-in-class tools are outright banned. Forcing everyone onto a half-baked, in-house knock-off.  Killing any hope of AI-driven productivity or innovation.

-Leadership Inertia & Resistance to Change: Senior leaders cling to make it happen. Inefficiencies persist year after year with no accountability. 

-Barriers to Internal Mobility: It's impossible to move internally. 

-Under-utilization of Technical Expertise: Despite strong skills in Python, API integrations, LLM fine-tuning, and automation, my day-to-day work is dominated by fiddling with shapes on slide decks instead of hands-on coding or data science. 

-Over-utilization of Offshore Resources: code and data-science work is pushed offshore, taking longer, more people, and constant revisions due to language barriers and misaligned goals. Yet leadership thinks it’s more efficient... despite AI upending that assumption. 

-Inadequate Compensation & Shrinking Bonuses: My annual raises barely keep pace with inflation, and everyone's PBB has shrunk for the past 3 years (at least). It’s demoralizing to see my total compensation decline in real terms.

-Repetitive, manual processes instead of scalable automation: We spend hours hand-editing tasks that could (and should) be automated end-to-end. The org defaults to manual because that’s what leadership understands.

-Bureaucratic Friction & Slow Decision-Making: Simple requests for project resources require multiple sign-offs and often stall. The red tape kills any prototyping, rapid iteration, and innovation.

-Good Benefits

cons:
This is one of the worst companies I've ever worked for. They are the epitome of the soul-less corporation. They talk about how important everyone is but then will fire you out of the blue if a single KPI is weak without consulting your leadership or even informing them. Their technology consulting strategy is to simply buy as many smaller consulting companies as they can and throw them all together and hope they somehow produce revenue. It's all a mess with goals constantly shifting, new nonsense initiatives rolled out every quarter, and constantly changing goalposts. They basically said if you want to get promoted you have to basically be doing that job for a year. It's wild.

There is a constant push to be contributing to 'the business' and internal projects, even if you are fully staffed and utilized. Doing your client work is considered the bare minimum and will likely get you passed over for promotions or maybe even fired. But if you do this extra credit you had better make sure your utilization numbers are still good. They also don't like it when you are overutilized. They want it both ways.

Most of the front line workers were great and a pleasure tow work with, but managers tended to be duplicitous and two-faced. Everyone is there to save their own skin and won't hesitate to pass the blame to you, warranted or not. I joined a project that had been going for three years in a role completely unfamiliar to me. Despite being very clear about this, my feedback provider still gave me a negative review on my feedback cycle a month later. Apparently I was not allowed any time to grow into the role.

I was there for 2 years and saw three rounds of layoffs. The third round they didn't even bother announcing. Not a healthy company if you ask me, just too big too fail.

EY was also supposed to do a big split between their Accounting and Consulting businesses. At the last second the American partners rebelled and all that work had to be thrown away. For some reason bonuses were weak that year. Can't figure it out... but seriously. Company is a mess and the firm structure makes it so that there are way too many cooks in the kitchen and there is no real clear direction for anything.

-Lots of layoffs
-Weak bonuses and raises while I was there
-Lots of layers of management (who are all not very good)
-Pay is not great (though they'll tell you its above average)
-Bad management
-Constantly changing goalposts
-Unorganized
-Ultra competitive
-Work can be demanding
-Managers will bend over backwards for clients at expense of team

-EY has lost clients in assurance. Be ready to be deployed to the Bay Area or another office in the US. You will have to travel to that office
- If nobody told you, it is expected from Sacramento Assurance staff to perform inventory counts in your two years as a staff. Be ready to drive through the Sierra Nevadas to get to Reno and all northen California. This will be specially occur during Christmas and New Year times. You will be on call for inventory counts so no break times to recharge. In the first year as staff, it was common to perform about 20 inventory counts. Second year less than 20 inventory counts for sure.
-Sacramento higher-ups (manager through executive director) play favorites games among employees. Be sure to watch your back for backstabbing. Trust nobody.
-There are no public clients in Sacramento. Only private entities and Uniform Guidance 
-Management will be monitoring your CPA progress from day.
- Your team and their review make greatly determine your success. You will have to become a person pleaser and will not be able to be yourself. EY molds you a certain way and you will have to be that way to progress
-Be ready for corporate mindset. You will have to show no emotions while you work in the office or few client sites.

pros:
-Great People! I was there for over 6 years because I loved the people there. This includes leadership and coworkers. Like every workplace, there's going to be some people you don't mesh well with, but overall, I appreciated the people
-Great Compensation and benefits! EY offers 2 week long "breaks" for the summer and winter, along with a lot of great benefits like fitness funds, Well Hub, and really competitive pay + annual pay increase structure. I could see how staying at EY long term would help set you and your family up financially long term.
cons:
-Difficulties moving between groups and within EY. This is something I did not anticipate when I first joined, but it's not the easiest place to move around between groups. Not only do you need to build a reputation between teams and groups, you also need to make sure your project start/end timelines align, which makes this really difficult. 
-I do feel that everyone, post COVID, seemed really stressed and overworked, including myself. Along with many others, I felt like I was constantly working, and it seemed like the Senior, Manager and SM classes were spread too thin. I don't know all the inner workings, so I can't propose a solution, but I can say that this is one of the primary reasons I resigned from EY.

title: EY is unwavering in its commitment to quality, prioritizing excellence above all else. The firm firmly believes that maintaining the integrity of its brand is paramount, even if it occasionally results in tighter margins due to misjudged efforts. This steadfast dedication ensures that clients receive the highest standard of work, reflecting EY's core values and reputation in the industry. Furthermore, this commitment to quality extends to the employees as well, fostering a work environment where talent is nurtured and encouraged to thrive. By placing quality at the forefront, EY not only upholds its legacy but also builds lasting relationships with clients and cultivates a motivated workforce dedicated to delivering exceptional results.
pros: EY is unwavering in its commitment to quality, prioritizing excellence above all else. The firm firmly believes that maintaining the integrity of its brand is paramount, even if it occasionally results in tighter margins due to misjudged efforts. This steadfast dedication ensures that clients receive the highest standard of work, reflecting EY's core values and reputation in the industry. Furthermore, this commitment to quality extends to the employees as well, fostering a work environment where talent is nurtured and encouraged to thrive. By placing quality at the forefront, EY not only upholds its legacy but also builds lasting relationships with clients and cultivates a motivated workforce dedicated to delivering exceptional results.
cons: One notable challenge at EY is the firm's aversion to a clearly defined hierarchy of management. This can complicate the decision-making process, as obtaining a firm resolution often requires consensus from multiple partner stakeholders. While this collaborative approach can foster diverse perspectives, it can also lead to delays and ambiguity, particularly when navigating complex issues. In this environment, it becomes evident that while all partners are considered equal, some may wield more influence than others, which can create an imbalance in the decision-making dynamics. As a result, employees may find themselves grappling with the intricacies of aligning various viewpoints, making it challenging to achieve timely and decisive outcomes.
